details.text: Derivative: رَبِّ is one of Allah’s names which refers to the heart of the relationship between Allah, the creator and His creation. Allah being the Rabb (master) dictates that we are His slaves (‘Ibaad).

Derivative: سَبِّحِ is the command form of سَبَّحَ – تَسْبِيح which means to remember or mention Allah with a certain profound remembrance that Allah Himself has taught. It comes from سِبَاحَة which refers to swimming in a manner that maintains the body above water and kind of resembles floating on the water’s surface. The linkage here in respect to remembering Allah and declaring His perfection is that floating signifies retaining a level and refusing to go down. As a result, we are declaring that Allah is beyond any imperfections, declare this level of perfection for Allah and we are not to mention Him in a manner that brings that level down and must acknowledge and maintain that perfection in respect to Him. The command of سَبِّحِ (Sabbih) can therefore mean: “Declare and maintain the perfection”. سَبِّحِ (Sabbih) is used in the Qur’an on numerous occasions but is followed by بِ (with) whilst here it is alone. This use of language can imply that the meaning here relates more to the acknowledgment of the perfection of Allah whilst when بِ (with) is used the remembrance and declaration of the perfection of Allah is implied more.

Derivative: الْأَعْلَى (The Most High) is a صِفَة (adjective) for رَبّ (Rabb – Lord/master) here i.e., “Declare and maintain the perfection of the name of your Master the Most High”. It comes from theWord: عَلَا – يَعْلُو – عُلُوٌّ which means to be high and height. It refers to height and elevation in terms of height and in grandeur.
Derivative: يَذَّكَّرُ is in origin يَتَذَّكَرُ which means to remember or be reminded i.e., will take heed.
Derivative: يَخْشَىٰ from خَشِيَ means to fear and relates to fearing something greater than oneself or has the power to overcome you.
Derivative: يَتَجَنَّبُ from تَجَنَّبَ – تَجَنُّب and it means to avoid, keep well away from and steer clear of.

Derivative: الْأَشْقَى comes from شَقِيَ – شَقَاوَة which means to be wretched, miserable and unfortunate. الْأَشْقَى is the superlative form (اِسْمُ التَّفْضِيل – Ismu t-Tafdeel) and means “the most wretched, miserable or unfortunate one”.
Derivative: تَزَكَّىٰ means to purify and cleanse oneself i.e., make effort to make oneself better.

Derivative: أَفْلَحَ – إِفْلاح originates from فَلاح (Falaah) which refers to a farmer when he reaps his harvest and attains the ultimate success from all the hard work that he has put in. أَفْلَحَ means to have attained success and implies a success that is attained as a result of hard work and many efforts. قَدْ أَفْلَحَ – The قَدْ before أَفْلَحَ means that it has already happened and is a done deal. قَدْ أَفْلَحَ therefore means: “Has already attained success”.

Derivative: تُؤْثِرُونَ from آَثَرَ – يُؤْثِرُ – إِيثار which means to give preference to something over another thing. It implies that two things are compared and then one is given preference over the other.

Derivative: الدُّنْيَا is the feminine form of أَدْنَى which means closer or inferior. Allah has named the life of this world as الدُّنْيَا (Dunyaa). The inferior material things of this world are seen to be close at hand whilst those exceedingly greater rewards of the hereafter (الآخِرَة – Al-Aakhirah) are seen as being distant.
Derivative: سَوَّىٰ – تَسْوِيَة which means to balance and even out. It refers to fashioning and moulding with precise proportions.
Derivative: الْمَرْعَىٰ comes from the verb رعَى which refers to grazing animals. مَرْعَى is a place and refers to the green pasture land where the shepherd grazes his animals.
Derivative: أَخْرَجَ – إِخْراج means to bring something out or extract it.

Derivative: غُثَاءً can refer to the leftovers and remnants of things like plants that gather or pile up on the surface of water or earth like froth, fragments, debris etc. It can also just refer to when plants gather and pile up next to each other in a jumble.

Derivative: أَحْوَىٰ comes from حَوَى which means to bring two things together. أَحْوَىٰ is used to describe when the colour of green veers towards the colour of black or red (i.e., dark colour) which can be a sign of maturity or of decay.

فَجَعَلَهُ غُثَاءً أَحْوَىٰ

- Due to the different implications that can be derived from the two main words of غُثَاءً and أَحْوَىٰ the verse can either allude to plants gathering and reaching maturity or to plants becoming withered, decaying and becoming remnants or debris that have lost their benefit. Either way it relates to plants and vegetation growing and reaching their culmination.
Derivative: نُقْرِئُ from أَقْرَأَ which means to make read i.e., Allah will provide with the capability to read and recite the Qur’an that comes to you.
Derivative: يَخْفَىٰ from خَفِيَ means to hide something so well that it is not known that it exists.
Derivative: شَاءَ to will or want and is used to refer to that which is based upon a concrete decision.

- Here Allah links His knowledge يَعْلَمُ (he knows) to الْجَهْرَ (what is open) which is a noun and then to what is hidden مَا يَخْفَىٰ which is a verb. The noun is usually used in Arabic to denote permanence whilst the verb is used for something that is not permanent. The implication is that which is hidden مَا يَخْفَىٰ does not remain hidden forever.
- In this statement إِنَّهُ “No doubt that He” يَعْلَمُ (He knows), the “He” is mentioned twice. The translation would therefore be: “It is in fact He who knows” and implies that no one else knows.

Derivative: الْيُسْرَىٰ (Al-Yusraa) is the superlative (اِسْمُ التَّفْضِيل – Ismu t-Tafdeel) feminine form of الأَيْسَر (Al-Aysar) and refers to the easiest or ultimate or greatest ease. This greatest ease has been interpreted here to relate to all his affairs, the path that he ﷺ is to follow to fulfil objectives and also to the Sharee’ah that he has or will be given that will bring ease to the people.

Derivative: نُيَسِّرُكَ
- نُيَسِّرُكَ is made up of نُيَسِّرُ “We will make easy” and كَ “You”. Usually, the two would be separated by لِ “for” i.e., نُيَسِّرُ لَكِ “We will make easy for you”. The purpose behind omitting the لِ “for” in the Arabic language here is to bring the two words closer together, and this is done here to demonstrate Allah’s closeness to His Messenger ﷺ.

Derivative: الذِّكْرَىٰ from ذَكَرَ – يَذْكُرُ – ذِكْرًا which means to mention or remember. ذِكْرَى is the hyperbolised form of ذِكْر (Dhikr) and means the ultimate or powerful reminder. It has also been said (in the classical dictionary Lisaan ul-‘Arab) that it can act as the Masdar (root noun) for three words derived from ذِكْر, which are: ذَكَرَ (Dhakara) ذَكَّرَ (Dhakkara)تَذَكَّرَ (Tadhakkara). It therefore can include the meanings of mentioning, remembering and making others remember or reminding them.
